coredata.dc:title: A dual-functional fluorescent sensor based on dihydropyrazole derivative for successive detection of Cu2+ and glyphosate and its applications
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xixian
You, Jun
Liu, Qiye
Liu, Bo
Yu, Yanchao
Wu, Wenju
coredata.dc:description: A novel “on-off-on” fluorogenic sensor DHPS based on dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone was devised for sequent Cu2+ and glyphosate detection in DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution. DHPS exhibited a linear range of 0.1–9 μM, a detection limit of 47 nM, and a highly selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response towards Cu2+. DHPS-Cu2+ complex showed excellent detection capability for glyphosate, a linear range of 0.5–11 μM, a detection limit of 52 nM, a wide pH range of 4.0 to 9.0, and a good selective and sensitive fluorescence recovery response. Besides, DHPS and DHPS-Cu2+ complex could detect Cu2+ and glyphosate in real water, respectively, and exhibited good stability and practicality, which provided some valuable ideas for the design of corresponding fluorescent probes in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone
Cu2+
Glyphosate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Identification performance of probe DHPS to Cu2+
Identification Performance of Probe DHPS to Cu2+
Anti-interference performance of probe DHPS with Cu2+
Fluorescence titration experiment of probe DHPS with Cu2+
Binding mode of probe DHPS and Cu2+
Identification performance of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with glyphosate
Selectivity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex for organophosphorus pesticides or anions
Anti-interference performance of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with glyphosate
Fluorescence titration experiment of DHPS-Cu2+ complex to glyphosate
Binding mode of DHPS-Cu2+ complex and glyphosate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the NSF of China (NO. 22278098, 21908034 and 22008045). We also thank the NSF of Heilongjiang Province (LH2021H001).
This work was financially supported by the NSF of China (NO. 22278098 , 21908034 and 22008045 ). We also thank the NSF of Heilongjiang Province ( LH2021H001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence intensity of DHPS (10 μM) in (a) different reagents and (b) DMSO buffer solutions (33 μM) (λex= 378 nm). (c) Fluorescence intensity change of DHPS, DHPS + Cu2+ and DHPS-Cu2+ + glyphosate in different pH conditions.

Fluorescence intensity (a) and UV–vis absorption spectra (b) of DHPS (10 μM) in the presence of different metal ions (33 μM) (λex= 378 nm); (c) Color changes of DHPS solution with different metal ions under natural light and (d) Fluorescence color changes of DHPS (10 μM) with different metal ions under portable UV lamp at 365 nm.
Anti-interference ability of probe DHPS (10 μM) recognition for Cu2+ in the presence of cations (a) and anions (b) (33 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of DHPS (10 μM) upon incremental addition of Cu2+ (0.1–14 μM) (λex=378 nm) and (b) linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 473 nm and concentration of Cu2+ (0.1–9 μM), Inset: Fluorescence intensity at 473 nm of DHPS (10 μM) upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0.1–14 μM).
(a) Job’s plot of DHPS with Cu2+; (b) FTIR spectra of DHPS and DHPS-Cu2+ complex.

Fluorescence intensity (a) and UV–vis absorption spectra (b) of DHPS-Cu2+ complex in the presence of different organophosphorus pesticides (33 μM); (c) Color changes of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with different organophosphorus pesticides under natural light and (d) Fluorescence color changes of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with different organophosphorus pesticides under portable UV lamp at 365 nm. 1# ∼ 14# correspond to probe DHPS, DHPS-Cu2+ complex, glyphosate, trichlorfon, phosmet, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, dimethoate, ethoprophos, fenitrothion, parathion methyl, parathion and glufosinate, respectively.
Anti-interference ability of DHPS-Cu2+ recognition for glyphosate (33 μM) in the presence of various OPs (a) and anions (b) (33 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of DHPS-Cu2+ complex upon addition of different concentrations of glyphosate (0.5–14 μM) and (b) linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 473 nm and concentration of glyphosate (0.5–11 μM); Inset: Fluorescence intensity at 473 nm of DHPS-Cu2+ complex upon addition of different concentrations of glyphosate (0.5–14 μM).
(a) Job’s plot of DHPS-Cu2+ with glyphosate; (b) Reusability test of the prepared DHPS sensor with an alternate flow of Cu2+ and glyphosate (denoted as Gly).
Proposed sequential recognition mechanism of DHPS for Cu2+ and glyphosate.
The synthetic route of probe DHPS.
Comparison of the fluorescent probe for Cu2+ and glyphosate detection.
Detection results of Cu2+ or glyphosate in actual water samples a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: There was no Cu2+ or glyphosate was detected in the actual sample.

head.ce:title: A dual-functional fluorescent sensor based on dihydropyrazole derivative for successive detection of Cu2+ and glyphosate and its applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering and Technology of College of Heilongjiang Province, College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin University of Science and Technology, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering and Technology of College of Heilongjiang Province, College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering and Technology of College of Heilongjiang Province, College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin University of Science and Technology, Harbin 150080, 150040, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel “on-off-on” fluorogenic sensor DHPS based on dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone was devised for sequent Cu2+ and glyphosate detection in DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution. DHPS exhibited a linear range of 0.1–9 μM, a detection limit of 47 nM, and a highly selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response towards Cu2+. DHPS-Cu2+ complex showed excellent detection capability for glyphosate, a linear range of 0.5–11 μM, a detection limit of 52 nM, a wide pH range of 4.0 to 9.0, and a good selective and sensitive fluorescence recovery response. Besides, DHPS and DHPS-Cu2+ complex could detect Cu2+ and glyphosate in real water, respectively, and exhibited good stability and practicality, which provided some valuable ideas for the design of corresponding fluorescent probes in the future.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescent probe DHPS based on dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone was established for sequent Cu2+ and glyphosate detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions are of vital significance in the fields of medical biology, environmental, material science and energy such as cancer treatment, plant photosynthesis, OLED devices, battery and electro-catalyst, etc. [1–4]. Among them, copper (Cu2+) ranks the third most abundant microelement after iron and zinc in the human body, which plays pleiotropic roles in numerous basic physiological processes like cellular respiration, ceruloplasmin and neurotransmitter generation [5–7]. However, deficiency and oversupply of Cu2+ may pose varied detrimental effects like Menkes syndrome, Alzheimer’s disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and mucosal necrosis [8–10]. EPA and WHO have set the maximum acceptable levels of 20.0 µM and 31.5 µM for Cu2+ ion in drinkable water, respectively [11,12]. Hence, there is an urgent necessity to establish highly sensitive and selective analytical methods to detect Cu2+.

Glyphosate is the most universally used nonselective herbicides globally, with an annual yield of around 600–750 thousand tons [13,14]. It is a momentous broad-spectrum and conductive aminophosphonic herbicide commonly distributed in agriculture, silviculture and graziery to prevent overgrowth of weed and vegetation [15–17]. However, the overuse of glyphosate is responsible for chemical residues in plants, soils and aquatic ecosystems that then enters living organisms through the food chain and accumulate toxicity, thereby seriously threatening public health like nervous system and reproductive health [18–22]. EPA and WHO have recommended the permissible residual levels of 4.1 µM and 5.3 µM for glyphosate in drinking water, respectively [23,24]. Therefore, it is imperative to exploit a prompt and reliable technicality for glyphosate sequential determination and quantification.

Enormous analytical techniques have been published for detecting Cu2+ or glyphosate, like chromatography, inductively coupled atomic absorption spectrometry and electrochemistry [25–27]. Compared with other pathways, fluorescent probes have been received extensive attention in real-time supervision of various metal ions or pesticides like Cu2+ or glyphosate owing to its outstanding merits of cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity and selectivity [28,29]. Noteworthily, dihydropyrazoles are important five-membered N-heterocycles, which are often worked as fluorescent materials because of their conjugacy, high fluorescence quantum yield, high hole transport efficiency and high selectivity [30–33]. They also have prosperous applications in fluorescent sensors that can selectively detect metal ions like Zn2+, Al3+ and Cu2+ [34]. Besides, salicylicaldehyde is an excellent synthetic intermediate for the manufacture of organic pigments and functional dyes, and oxygen atom (from hydroxyl group (-OH)) in its structure can coordinate with Cu2+, leading to a change in fluorescence intensity [35]. So far, a few works have been built for Cu2+ and glyphosate fluorometric recognition [36–39]. However, it has been unreported to successively detect Cu2+ and glyphosate using a single fluorescent probe based on dihydropyrazole derivative and salicylicaldehyde. This has inspired us to introduce 4-diethylaminosalicylic aldehyde (as the electron-donating group) into dihydropyrazole-derived core to form a Schiff-base compound with conjugated moieties and CN double bonds, giving fluorescence emission and bonding ability concurrently [40]. Thus, it is highly desirable to develop a new “on-off-on” highly selective fluorescent sensor and further broaden its application sectors.

Motivated by the above considerations, we designed a new fluorescent probe DHPS based on dihydropyrazole derivative and 4-diethylaminosalicylic aldehyde for successive Cu2+ and glyphosate detection ( Scheme 1). Fluorescence “on-off-on” method had unique characteristics for sensing Cu2+ and glyphosate like good selectivity, anti-interference ability and sensitivity. More importantly, we used DHPS to directly detect copper ions and used DHPS-Cu2+ complex to strictly detect glyphosate in real water. Current method provided some valuable ideas for Cu2+ and glyphosate related fields like environmental pollution or disease diagnosis.

Preparation of nitrile imine and 1-acryloyl-3,5-dimethylpyrazole olefin was described in the references [41,42]. All chemicals and reagents (Hydrazine hydrate (80w%), 4-diethylaminosalicylic aldehyde, 1,2-Dichloroethane (DCE), Methylene chloride (DCM), Ethanol) were derived from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. that were used directly without further purification. The standard solutions of organophosphorus pesticides were acquired from Shanghai ANPEL Laboratory Technologies Inc. The water used throughout the experiments was doubly distilled water.

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker Avance III apparatus (300 and 75 MHz). IR spectra were done with a Thermo Nicolet 370 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. Elemental analyses (C, H, and N) were conducted by a Perkin-Elmer 2400 CHN elemental analyzer. Mass (MS) spectra were performed on a Waters G2-XS QTof mass spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-4500 spectrometer. Data of absorption spectrum were collected with a Thermo T600UV–VIS spectrophotometer. Melting points were carried out on a Tektronix X-6 Micromelting point detector.

The synthetic route of probe DHPS was depicted in Scheme 1, and the synthetic procedure and spectra data of compounds 1 and 2 were described in the supporting literature (Fig. S1-S10). Initially, 2 (1.49 g, 4.14 mmol) and 4-diethylaminosalicylic aldehyde (0.8 g, 4.55 mmol) were dissolved in 45 mL ethanol and refluxed for 5 h. Later, the resulting solid was cooled to ambient temperature, filtered, air-dried, and recrystallized from DCM to obtain 1.88 g of probe DHPS as a pale-yellow powder with 85% yield. mp. 232.0–232.6 °C. Corresponding data and spectra were shown in the supporting literature (Fig. S11-S14).

All practical solutions (10 mM) of metal ions (Zn2+, K+, Bi2+, Cu2+, Na+, Ni2+, Cs+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Li+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Ag+), anions (Cr2O7 2-, S2O3 2-, HCO3 -, Cl-, NO3 2-, HSO4 -, SO4 2-, CH3COO-, I-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, Br-, F-, CO3 2-) and organophosphorus pesticides (glyphosate, trichlorfon, phosmet, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, dimethoate, ethoprophos, fenitrothion, parathion methyl, parathion, glufosinate) were garnered by dissolving their saline or compounds in deionized water. The solution (10 μM) of probe DHPS was prepared in DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution. Each experiment was performed at least in triplicate, in addition, all experiments of fluorescence emission spectra were performed at ambient temperature with 378 nm of excitation wavelengths and 5.0 nm of excitation slit width. UV absorption and fluorescence spectra were done by successively adding the relevant substances with universal pipette guns.

To test the practical application capability of DHPS and DHPS-Cu2+ complex, the water from Tap water and Songhua River was selected, the water samples were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min and filtered by 0.45 µm filter membrane twice to remove the un-dissolved substances before detection. The gained specimens were spiked with different amounts of copper ions or glyphosate and tested according to the standard addition method.

Specifically, 3 mL of DHPS or DHPS-Cu2+ solution (10 μM) was taken, and different volumes (3 µL, 6 µL,., 30 µL) of Cu2+ or glyphosate solution were added, respectively. The reaction was allowed to conduct for the same duration and then fluorescence spectra were tested.

Job’s plot experiments were carried out under identical conditions, varying proportions of reactants and analytes were added and the total concentration remained unchanged at 10 μM throughout the experiment. Fluorescence intensity was then measured as the molar ratio was varied from 0 to 1.

A (privileged structural Schiff-base) acylhydrazone-based fluorescent probe was constructed as a donor-acceptor structure by the condensation reaction of dihydropyrazole derivative with 4-diethylaminosalicylic aldehyde. Dihydropyrazole derivatives were commonly used as fluorescent materials due to their good conjugation, outstanding photostability and high fluorescence quantum yield. Meanwhile, 4-diethylaminosalicylic aldehyde had fluorescence properties and a strong binding site of oxygen atom (from hydroxyl (-OH)) for coordinating with Cu2+. Acylhydrazone compounds coordinated with metal ions to form the rigid plane and D-π-A conjugate connections with fluorescence characteristics [43]. The imino, carbonyl, hydroxyl and diethylamino groups acted as electron donors [44], and the phenyl, p-bromobenzene and dihydropyrazole rings segments served as electron acceptors, designing a novel fluorescence probe based on dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone for Cu2+ and glyphosate detection.

Solvent polarity played an imperative role in the photophysical properties of fluorescent sensor [45]. To determine the optimum condition, we scrutinized fluorescence emission spectra of probe DHPS in different pure reagents and buffer solutions, as revealed in Fig. 1. When the excitation wavelength of 378 nm, fluorescence emission intensity of DHPS in DMSO reagent showed the strongest fluorescence emission peak at 473 nm compared to other reagents including tetrahydrofuran, DMF, methanol, acetonitrile and ethanol (Fig. 1a). After that, fluorescence intensity of DHPS in 95:5 DMSO buffer solution was stronger than other proportions of DMSO buffer solution (Fig. 1b).

Acidity (pH) was another important parameter that directly affected the probe responses. Fluorescent spectrum of DHPS with or without Cu2+ and glyphosate were delved under 95:5 DMSO buffer solution with different pH values (Fig. 1c). At pH less than 3.0, it was weak fluorescence emission changed for DHPS, which suggested that DHPS was not suitable for strong acidic medium. DHPS showed a usable and stronger fluorescence intensity at pH 4.0 to 9.0, and a distinct fluorescence quenching was observed as DHPS complexed with Cu2+. The addition of glyphosate to DHPS-Cu2+ complex promoted fluorescence intensity of the solution to be basically consistent with ligand DHPS. Fluorescence intensity of DHPS was conspicuously enhanced under strong alkaline conditions (pH greater than 10.0), which may be caused by the destruction of DHPS structure. Results implied that DHPS could be utilized as a candidate for detecting environmental Cu2+ and glyphosate under the range of pH 4.0 to 9.0 and experimental condition was selected for a physiological pH of 7.4. Therefore, DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution was adopted throughout the spectral experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification performance of probe DHPS to Cu2+
Identification Performance of Probe DHPS to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the selectivity of DHPS toward Cu2+, fluorescence spectra and UV absorption for a sequence of common metal ions (Zn2+, K+, Bi2+, Cu2+, Na+, Ni2+, Cs+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Li+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Ag+) and anions (Cr2O7 2-, S2O3 2-, HCO3 -, Cl-, NO3 2-, HSO4 -, SO4 2-, CH3COO-, I-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, Br-, F-, CO3 2-) were conducted in DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution ( Fig. 2 and S15). DHPS showed strong fluorescence emission band centered at 473 nm, anions and most cations showed negligible fluorescence change, while Ce3+ cation induced slight fluorescence quenching, likely because the acting force of Ce3+ with DHPS was right in the middle of Cu2+ and other metal ions [46]. Fluorescence intensity dramatically declined from 5812 to 92 a.u. after the addition of Cu2+, reaching 98.4% quenching efficiency (Fig. 2a and S15a). Results corroborated that DHPS displayed superior selectivity of Cu2+, which could be attributed to that Cu2+ ion had higher binding affinity and faster chelating process with “N” and “O” groups on the surface of DHPS than other ions [47]. As illustrated in Fig. 2b and S15b, DHPS exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 378 nm, nevertheless, DHPS-Cu2+ complex showed a very well-defined absorption peak at 392 nm with a red shift of 26 nm when Cu2+ existed, in contrast, addition of other metal ions and anions did not induce any apparent absorption changes. Fluorescent selectivity of DHPS for Cu2+ was also investigated by the visualized color change (Figs. 2c, d and S16). The fluorescence quenching phenomenon was observed and fluorescence color of the solution changed from bright blue to transparent under 365 nm ultraviolet light (color of the solution changed visually from yellow to transparent) after adding Cu2+, while the color and fluorescence color of probe solution no changed after adding other ions. Thus, experimental results demonstrated that DHPS was more selective to Cu2+ than other competitive ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference performance of probe DHPS with Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify its selectivity, DHPS was subjected to the interfering study under the coexistence of cations and anions. Figs. 3a and b showed an instant decrease of fluorescence intensity upon the existence of Cu2+. Fluorescence intensity of DHPS solution remained unchanged when other cations or anions were added, which indicated that the recognition of Cu2+ by DHPS at 473 nm was undisturbed even in the presence of interfering substances. Results further implemented that DHPS had a promising selectivity for the detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence titration experiment of probe DHPS with Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensitivity of DHPS toward Cu2+, fluorescence titration experiments were performed at different concentrations of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4a, fluorescence intensity of DHPS at 473 nm decreased progressively as the content of Cu2+ ions grew (0.1 to 14 μM). When continued addition of Cu2+ (> 14 μM), fluorescence intensity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex had no discernible change and DHPS had reached complexation equilibrium with Cu2+. Fig. 4b showcased a good linear relationship (Y=−544.25X+5710.54, R2 =0.9965, X was the concentration of Cu2+ ions) between fluorescence intensity at 473 nm and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.1 to 9 μM. According to the calculation formula of detection limit: LOD= 3σ/k (σ and k were standard deviation of the maximum fluorescence intensity of the identical probe sample measured 10 times and slope of the linear equation in the fluorescence titration test, respectively), the calculated determination limit LOD of DHPS for Cu2+ was 47 nM. Results indicated that DHPS exhibited a high detection sensitivity for Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding mode of probe DHPS and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the complexation ratio of Cu2+ with DHPS, Job’s plot analysis was proceeded ( Fig. 5a). The maximum emission was found when the molar fraction of Cu2+ was 0.53, which suggested the complexation ratio of probe DHPS and Cu2+ ion was 1:1. Fluorescence titration data in Fig. 4a was linearly fitted, and then the equilibrium constant Ka= 1.08 × 105 M−1 was calculated according to the Benesi-Hildebrand plot [48], as showed in Fig. S17. Besides, by comparing FTIR spectra of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with that of probe DHPS (Fig. 5b), DHPS was converted from keto-form to enol-form to participate in the coordination with Cu2+. The characteristic absorption peaks observed for DHPS emerged at 3431, 1670 and 1631 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H, CO and CN groups, respectively. For DHPS-Cu2+ complex, the peak located at 3431 cm−1 disappeared, and 1670 cm−1 peak shifted to 1621 cm−1 referring to the stretching vibrations of two overlapping CN (Fig. S13 and S18). FTIR spectra revealed that multiple binding sites of two oxygen atoms (from enol-form hydroxyl (-OH) and phenolic hydroxyl (-OH)) and one nitrogen atom (from CN) of probe DHPS for strong complexing with Cu2+, forming a stable DHPS-Cu2+ complex and causing fluorescence quenching [49–51]. Meanwhile, the formation of DHPS-Cu2+ complex was further validated by CHN elemental analysis and high-resolution mass spectrometry (Fig. S19), and it was found that the ion peak m/z after DHPS-Cu2+ complex was 595.0646, basically consistent with the theoretical value 595.0644. These results suggested a 1:1 stoichiometry for DHPS-Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification performance of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with glyphosate
Selectivity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex for organophosphorus pesticides or anions
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the selectivity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex toward glyphosate, fluorescence spectra and UV absorption for various organophosphorus pesticides (glyphosate, trichlorfon, phosmet, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, dimethoate, ethoprophos, fenitrothion, parathion methyl, parathion, glufosinate) and anions were performed in DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution ( Fig. 6 and S20). After adding pesticides or anions (1.0 equiv.), the others barely exerted any effects in fluorescence intensity, except glyphosate (Fig. 6a and S20a). Though the structures of glufosinate and glyphosate were very similar, the methyl and amino groups in glufosinate hindered its spatial chelation with Cu2+, resulting in its inability to resume DHPS-Cu2+ complex fluorescence [52]. Following the addition of glyphosate, the fluorescence of DHPS-Cu2+ complex was almost restored to fluorescent intensity of probe DHPS alone. This was attributed to glyphosate by the removal of Cu2+ from DHPS-Cu2+ complex, releasing free DHPS and causing significant fluorescence recovery [53–55]. Then, we added Cu2+ to DHPS/Cu2+-glyphosate solution and found that the fluorescence disappeared again, which means the complex between probe DHPS and Cu2+ formed again. After glyphosate was added to DHPS-Cu2+ solution, resulting in a blue shift of the maximum UV absorption peak, which was basically consistent with the absorption spectra of DHPS (Fig. 6b and S20b). It was reconfirmed that glyphosate could snatch Cu2+ from DHPS-Cu2+ complex and release ligand DHPS. In addition, only in the presence of glyphosate, DHPS-Cu2+ complex solution color and fluorescence color changed from transparent to yellow and from transparent to bright blue under natural light and UV lamp at 365 nm, respectively, and the rest no colors and fluorescence colors changed (Figs. 6c, d and S21). Results demonstrated that DHPS-Cu2+ complex enabled unambiguous detection of glyphosate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference performance of DHPS-Cu2+ complex with glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: To further attest its selectivity, DHPS-Cu2+ complex was subjected to the interfering study under the coexistence of organophosphorus pesticides and anions ( Fig. 7). Fluorescence intensity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex at 473 nm did not change when other organophosphorus pesticides and anions added, except glyphosate. Once glyphosate participated, fluorescence intensities of all systems were significantly enhanced, indicating that DHPS-Cu2+ complex for fluorescence recognition of glyphosate was not interfered with the coexistence of organophosphorus pesticides and anions. Results uncovered that DHPS-Cu2+ complex had good specific recognition and anti-interference ability for the detection of glyphosate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence titration experiment of DHPS-Cu2+ complex to glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the sensitivity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex for glyphosate detection, we performed spectral concentration titration tests ( Fig. 8a). Fluorescence intensity of DHPS-Cu2+ complex gradually increased and returned to the initial state with rising glyphosate concentrations. A good linear relationship between DHPS-Cu2+ complex fluorescence intensity and concentration of glyphosate in the range of 0.5–11 μM was got and showed in Fig. 8b. The linear regression equation was Y= 398.56X+ 187.01, and the linear correlation coefficient was R2 = 0.9974. The detection limit LOD of DHPS-Cu2+ complex for glyphosate sensing was 52 nM according to the calculation formula of detection limit: LOD= 3σ/k. Compared with other appeared fluorescent sensing systems for Cu2+ and glyphosate ( Table 1), this tactic showed a preponderance in sensitivity. Results denoted DHPS and DHPS-Cu2+ complex had high sensitivity for Cu2+ and glyphosate detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding mode of DHPS-Cu2+ complex and glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: The coordination ratio of DHPS-Cu2+ complex and glyphosate was executed by Job’s plot experiments. When the molar fraction of glyphosate appeared at 0.51 ( Fig. 9a), the DHPS-Cu2+ solution had the maximum fluorescence intensity at 473 nm and the complexation ratio of Cu2+ to DHPS was confirmed previously to be 1:1, which verified that the complexation ratio between Cu2+ and glyphosate was 1:1.

To test the response reversibility, the reversible sensing behavior of DHPS was proceeded by fluorescence emission spectroscopy over five consecutive cycles by the addition of Cu2+ (1.0 equiv.) followed by glyphosate (1.0 equiv.). as discussed in Fig. 9b. This indicated a reversible interaction between DHPS-Cu2+ and glyphosate and also played a supporting role in explaining the following fluorescence mechanism.

Combined with the detection mechanism of several existing fluorescent probes based on dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone and the measured experimental data to explore possible fluorescence mechanism [61]. The acylhydrazone framework served as a privileged structural Schiff-base motif with good rigid coplanar structure and large π-conjugated system in probe DHPS compound, conferring fluorescence characteristics. Multiple binding sites of two oxygen atoms (from enol-form hydroxyl (-OH) and hydroxyl (-OH)) and one nitrogen atom (from imino) in probe DHPS were converted from keto-form to enol-form for strong complexing with Cu2+, forming a stable DHPS-Cu2+ complex and causing fluorescence quenching [62,63]. In the meantime, strong multiple binding sites of two oxygen atoms (from phosphonyl (-PO(OH)2) and carboxyl (-COOH)) and one nitrogen atom (from amidogen) of glyphosate for strong complexing with Cu2+ and fast displacement of glyphosate for Cu2+ from DHPS-Cu2+ complex to release ligand DHPS, causing fluorescence recovery [64,65]. The inferred mechanism of probe DHPS continuous detection of Cu2+ and glyphosate was elucidated in Fig. 10.

To evaluate the feasibility and applicability of fluorescence “on-off-on” method on the environment condition, laboratory tap water and Songhua River water were chosen for spiked experiments ( Table 2). Current method had the respective recovery rates of 98.89%− 104.43% and 99.09%− 105.90%, and respective relative standard deviations of 0.72%− 2.67% and 0.73%− 3.65%. The added standard concentration was well matched with the measured concentration. Results manifested that DHPS and DHPS-Cu2+ complex had higher precision and accuracy in detecting Cu2+ and glyphosate in actual water samples, rendering some advice for Cu2+ and glyphosate related aspects like environmental surveillance or food inspection.

In summary, we rationally built a simple yet new dihydropyrazole-derived acylhydrazone type sensor DHPS acting as a reversible “on-off-on” fluorescent response for Cu2+ and glyphosate in DMSO/H2O (95:5, v/v, HEPES 20 mM, pH=7.4) solution. Under the optimized conditions, fluorescence “on-off-on” method with simplicity in preparation and manipulation exhibited the detection limits for Cu2+ and glyphosate were 47 nM and 52 nM, respectively. Furthermore, we utilized DHPS to directly detect copper ions and used DHPS-Cu2+ complex to successfully detect glyphosate in real water. Accordingly, this work presented an example of dihydropyrazole segment with bifunctional detection and showed some dependable hints to construct “on-off-on” fluorescent materials with high valuable.
Liu Qiye: Investigation. You Jun: Writing – review & editing. Cao Xixian: Writing – original draft. Wu Wenju: Supervision. Yu Yanchao: Writing – review & editing. Liu Bo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the NSF of China (NO. 22278098, 21908034 and 22008045). We also thank the NSF of Heilongjiang Province (LH2021H001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.107975.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
